---
title: "Patterns in Time: Trend, Seasonality, and Noise"
unit: "V"
lesson: "13"
type: concept
tags: [time-series, resampling, rolling-mean, seasonality, trend, normalization]
difficulty: introductory
duration: "50 mins"
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

- **Describe** what makes time-series data different — order carries information, timestamps become the index — and **identify** the three components of a series: trend, seasonality, noise. *(Understand)*
- **Choose** a temporal scale (resampling daily to monthly to annual) appropriate to a question and **justify** the information trade-off. *(Apply / Analyze)*
- **Identify** trend with rolling means, and seasonality and periodicity with monthly climatologies and lag correlation. *(Apply)*
- **Apply** normalization — z-scores across units, anomalies against a baseline — to compare series and to separate trend from the seasonal cycle. *(Apply)*

> **Where this sits:** after **L12** mapped patterns in *space* · this lesson is patterns in *time* · next, L14 closes Unit V with patterns across *many variables*.

> **Hands-on:** [Lab — Sixty-Five Years of San Juan Weather](l13_lab_san_juan_climate.md) ·
> [![Open in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/{{< var repo.org >}}/{{< var repo.name >}}/blob/{{< var repo.branch >}}/u05_visualization/l13_lab_san_juan_climate.ipynb)

## Why This Matters

On September 20, 2017, the rain gauge at San Juan's Luis Muñoz Marín
airport recorded 180.6 mm of rain — and then the thermometer went silent
for two days. Hurricane María is five rows in a table that the station has
been writing since 1960: one row per day, 23,742 rows, longer than anyone
in the classroom has been alive.

A table like that answers almost nothing by inspection. Is San Juan
getting hotter? Is there a rainy season? Was María the worst day? Each of
those questions is about a *pattern that fluctuates in time*, and each
pattern lives at a different time scale — single days, the cycle of a
year, the drift of decades. The temporal toolkit is small — change the
scale, smooth, group by season, normalize — but with it, one thermometer
tells sixty-five years of history:

![Line chart of San Juan monthly temperature anomalies from 1960 to 2024. A pale blue jagged line shows monthly departures from each month's normal value, and a thick red 12-month rolling mean snakes through it, starting near minus one degree Celsius in the 1960s and climbing irregularly to plus one and a half degrees by 2024.](assets/l13_anomaly_trend.png)

**Reading the output:** this is where the lab ends up — the seasonal cycle
subtracted away, the noise smoothed away, and what remains is the long-term
story: San Juan's months now run about **1.5 C warmer** than they did when
the station started watching. None of that is visible in the raw table.
That is what this lesson's tools are for.

## What Is a Time Series?

> **Analogy:** a regular table is a deck of index cards — shuffle it and
> nothing is lost. A time series is a flip-book: each page barely differs
> from the last, and the *order is the story*. Shuffle a flip-book and you
> have confetti.

A **time series** is a sequence of observations where each value is
attached to a **timestamp** and the *order carries information*: today
resembles yesterday, Septembers resemble Septembers. In pandas this is a
Series or DataFrame whose **index holds the timestamps** — which is what
unlocks date slicing (`.loc["2017-09"]`), resampling, and rolling windows.

The working mental model of this lesson: a series is the sum of three
components.

| Component | What it is | San Juan example |
|---|---|---|
| **Trend** | the slow drift across the whole record | warming of about +1.5 C since the 1960s |
| **Seasonality** | a wave that repeats at a *fixed, known* period | warm Augusts, cool Januaries — every year |
| **Noise** | day-to-day jitter left over; includes one-off events | a 180.6 mm hurricane day |

The cleanest way to see the model is to *build* a series — then there is
no doubt about the truth, because we wrote it down:

```python
rng = np.random.default_rng(4001)
months = pd.date_range("2019-01-31", periods=72, freq="ME")
trend = np.linspace(0, 1.5, 72)                            # slow climb
season = 1.2 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * (months.month - 4) / 12) # 12-month wave
noise = rng.normal(0, 0.35, 72)                            # seeded jitter
observed = pd.Series(25 + trend + season + noise, index=months)
```

![Four stacked line charts. The top panel, labeled observed equals trend plus season plus noise, shows a wavy series climbing slowly. Below it, three panels show each ingredient alone: a straight rising trend line in red, a clean repeating 12-month sine wave in blue, and structureless gray jitter labeled noise.](assets/l13_components.png)

**Reading the output:** the top panel is what a real dataset hands you;
the three panels below are what it is *made of*. Real data never comes
with its components labeled — the rest of this lesson is the toolkit for
recovering each one. Two vocabulary notes: when a cycle's length is fixed
by the calendar we call it **seasonality**; when a repeating cycle has to
be *discovered from the data*, we speak of **periodicity** (and below we
will discover one without using the calendar at all).

## The Scale You Choose Decides What You See

The first tool is almost embarrassingly simple: change the resolution.
**Resampling** regroups a series into coarser time bins — days into
months, months into years — and aggregates each bin to one value:

```python
temp_m = weather["TMID"].resample("ME").mean()   # monthly mean temperature
rain_m = weather["PRCP"].resample("ME").sum()    # monthly TOTAL rainfall
```

The aggregation is a decision, not a default. Temperature is a *level*, so
a month is summarized by its **mean**; rainfall *accumulates*, so a month
is its **sum** — average the daily rain instead and a hurricane month full
of dry days looks unremarkable. Now watch one thermometer at three scales:

![Three stacked line charts of the same San Juan temperature record. The daily panel is a dense solid ribbon of 23,742 points. The monthly-means panel resolves into a clean wave repeating every year. The annual-means panel, drawn in red, shows a slowly climbing line from about 26 degrees to over 28 degrees Celsius.](assets/l13_three_scales.png)

**Reading the output:** same data, three different stories. The daily
ribbon shows noise and single events; the monthly wave is the seasonal
cycle; the annual line is the trend. **Each pattern is visible at exactly
one scale and invisible at the others** — choosing the scale *is* choosing
the question.

::: {.callout-important title="Coarsening is lossy"}
Resampling buys clarity by throwing information away — and it does not
warn you about what it discarded. At the annual scale, Hurricane María
does not exist: 2017's rain total is unremarkable. If the question is
about events, stay at (or return to) the fine scale; report which scale
and which aggregation you chose, because a reader cannot reconstruct
either from the plot.
:::

## Finding the Trend

A monthly series still mixes the seasonal wave with the long-term drift.
The **rolling mean** (moving average) separates them: replace each point
with the average of a **window** centered on it.

```python
roll12 = temp_m.rolling(window=12, center=True).mean()
```

The window size is the dial. Make it exactly one seasonal cycle — 12
months — and the wave averages to the same value everywhere, vanishing
from the output; what survives is the drift. The naive instinct is "more
smoothing is better": a 3-month window leaves the wave half-alive, while a
20-year window flattens real decade-scale swings into a featureless line.
The better rule: **match the window to the pattern you want to remove**
(one full cycle), and say so. In the lab this yields the lesson's headline:
the 1960s averaged 26.17 C, the last decade 27.65 C — and the warmest year
in the record is 2024.

## Finding the Seasons

Seasonality repeats on the calendar, so use the calendar against it: pool
every January from 1960 to 2024, every February, and so on. The twelve
group means are the **climatology** — the station's "normal year":

```python
temp_clim = temp_m.groupby(temp_m.index.month).mean()
```

San Juan's normal year swings just **3.2 C** between January (25.2 C) and
August (28.4 C) — the tropics have seasons, only quieter — while rain has
a sharper shape: a dry March, a November peak near 170 mm.

The climatology *assumed* the cycle was 12 months long. **Periodicity** is
the same idea without the assumption: let the data reveal its own cycle.
The instrument is one you already own — L08's correlation coefficient,
computed between the series and a time-shifted copy of itself, the
**autocorrelation**:

```python
acf = [temp_m.autocorr(lag) for lag in range(1, 25)]
```

For San Juan's monthly temperature: **+0.87 at lag 12** (this August looks
like last August), **-0.60 at lag 6** (August is February's opposite), and
a second peak at lag 24. That up-down-up signature *is* a 12-month cycle,
detected without ever mentioning the calendar.

## Making Series Comparable

Units sabotage comparison. Plot monthly rainfall (tens to hundreds of mm)
against monthly temperature (24 to 30 C) on one axis and the temperature
flattens into a ribbon — the axis belongs to whichever series has the
bigger numbers. **Normalization** puts series on a common scale; two moves
cover this course:

- **z-score** — re-express each value as standard deviations from the
  series' own mean: $z = (x - \bar{x}) / s$, with L07's mean and standard
  deviation as the ruler. Both series now swing around 0 in the same
  units of "how unusual". (The other classic, **min-max** scaling to
  [0, 1], compresses everything between the observed extremes — fine for
  bounded gauges, fragile when outliers set the bounds.)
- **anomaly** — normalize against the *calendar*: subtract from each month
  its own climatological normal. What remains is "how unusual *for that
  month*" — the seasonal cycle is gone by construction, and the trend
  stands alone. This is how climate results are reported, usually against
  a fixed 30-year baseline (1961-1990 is the WMO classic).

The z-score also works as an extremeness ruler within one series: María's
September 2017 ranks **z = +3.6** among all months of rain since 1960 —
enormous, yet not the record (August 2011 reached +4.5).

## Removing Noise

The third component needs less machinery than the first two: **smoothing**
*is* the rolling mean again, with the window matched to the noise you want
gone — 7 days to quiet day-to-day jitter, 30 for weather systems, 365 for
everything but the trend.

::: {.callout-warning title="Noise removal removes events"}
Smoothing cannot tell jitter from history. San Juan's all-time heat record
(36.7 C) survives a 7-day average at 35.6, fades to 33.6 at a month, and
has vanished entirely from the 365-day curve (31.7). The hurricane and the
heat wave live in exactly the component smoothing deletes. Use the
simplest smoothing that achieves clarity — and keep the raw series in
reach.
:::

## When to Use Which

- **Raw daily resolution** — when events and extremes are the story: the
  180.6 mm day, the record high. Patterns drown in it; events live in it.
- **Monthly scale + climatology** — for seasonal questions: when is the
  rainy season, how big is the swing.
- **Annual scale, rolling means, anomalies** — for trend questions: is it
  getting hotter. The anomaly view is the strongest, because it removes
  the seasonal cycle instead of just averaging over it.
- **No time plot at all** — when order does not matter for the question.
  "How hot do San Juan days get?" is a histogram (L07), not a time series.

## Common Pitfalls

- **Reading trend from a short window.** Two warm years is weather, not a
  trend — the lab's red rolling line wanders for a decade at a time.
  Climate normals use 30 years for a reason; quote the span your claim
  rests on.
- **Wrong aggregation for the variable.** Means for levels (temperature),
  sums for amounts (rain). `resample("ME").mean()` on rainfall makes
  hurricane months look ordinary.
- **Claiming seasonality from under two cycles.** One year of data shows
  one winter — you cannot tell the wave from a one-off. Two full periods
  is the floor; more is better.
- **Comparing raw series across units.** The bigger numbers own the axis
  and the smaller series flatlines. Normalize first, and name the method.
- **Smoothing past the events.** Every widening window shaves the
  extremes; by 365 days the record high is gone. Smoothing is for seeing
  trend, never for reporting events.

## Quiz Hooks

*Feeds the retrieval quiz at the start of the next session.*

- Given short descriptions of three features of a plotted series (a slow multi-year climb; a wave repeating every 12 months; leftover jitter), match each to trend, seasonality, or noise <!-- obj 1 · Understand · Moodle MC -->
- An analyst resamples daily rainfall to monthly with `.mean()` and reports the hurricane month looks normal; diagnose the aggregation mistake and name the fix <!-- obj 2 · Apply · Moodle MC -->
- A monthly series correlates +0.87 with itself at lag 12 and -0.60 at lag 6; pick the correct interpretation <!-- obj 3 · Apply · Moodle MC -->
- Temperature in C and rainfall in mm must share one comparison; pick the right move (z-score both) and reject the distractors (raw overlay; resample finer; correlation matrix) <!-- obj 4 · Apply · Moodle MC -->

## FAQ / Industry Reality

**"Is +1.5 C at one airport proof of global warming?"** — Careful. One
station is one thermometer: instruments get replaced, the airport grew
around the sensor (cities run warm), and siting changes leave fingerprints.
Climate science rests on *networks* of stations, cross-checked and
homogenized, and reports anomalies against fixed baselines. This station's
trend is consistent with the regional picture — but the honest claim from
this lab is about San Juan's record, not the planet.

**"Is this forecasting?"** — No, and the difference matters
professionally. Everything in this lesson *describes the past*: trend,
season, and noise are read out of data that already happened. Forecasting
models (ARIMA, exponential smoothing, Prophet) build on exactly these
components but add machinery for projecting them forward with uncertainty
— later courses' territory. Describe before you predict.

**"Why not just smooth everything and show the clean line?"** — Because
the clean line is a claim: "the wiggles I deleted were noise." Sometimes
they were a hurricane. Professionals show the raw series *behind* the
smoothed one (as the lab's figures do), report the window, and pick the
mildest smoothing that makes the pattern visible.

## Cheat-sheet

| Task | Call |
|---|---|
| Make timestamps the index | `pd.read_csv(..., parse_dates=["DATE"]).set_index("DATE")` |
| Slice by date | `s.loc["2017-09"]` · `s.loc["2015":"2024"]` |
| Coarsen the scale | `s.resample("ME").mean()` (levels) · `.sum()` (amounts) |
| Fill gaps, time-aware | `s.interpolate(method="time")` |
| Trend (cancel the seasons) | `s.rolling(12, center=True).mean()` |
| The normal year | `s.groupby(s.index.month).mean()` |
| Detect a period | `s.autocorr(lag)` across lags |
| Compare across units | z-score: `(s - s.mean()) / s.std()` |
| De-seasonalize | `s - clim[s.index.month].values` |

## Where to Go Deeper

- **Official docs:** [pandas — Time series / date functionality](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/timeseries.html) — resampling, rolling windows, and date slicing, with every frequency code this lesson used.
- **The data source:** [NOAA NCEI — Global Historical Climatology Network Daily](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/land-based-station/global-historical-climatology-network-daily) — what the station network measures and how the archive is quality-controlled.
- **The classic:** E. Tufte, *The Visual Display of Quantitative Information* — the principles behind why the anomaly plot persuades and the raw ribbon does not (course bibliography).
